summary: DHH notes that shortly after Ruby on Rails was released, developers in multiple communities—Tcl, Python, and .NET—began attempting to recreate Rails in their own languages. He finds this flattering but puzzling, arguing that learning Ruby and using the original Rails would be far less work than porting the entire framework. He points to examples of developers picking up Ruby quickly and being productive in hours.
key_points: Multiple language communities (Tcl, Python, .NET) immediately tried to clone Rails after its release
DHH argues learning Ruby is far easier than recreating Rails in another language
Early Rails adoption stories showed developers becoming productive within hours of learning Ruby
The impulse to rewrite rather than adopt reveals how language loyalty can override pragmatism
money_quotes: It's certainly flattering if only a little befuddling: Why not try the real thing out for size first?
I promise you it'll be a lot less work to Getting Started with Ruby and running on Rails than it will be to recreate the sensation in another language on another platform.
Ruby on Rails is here, today, offering the rapid development experience free of charge.
key_insight: Developers' instinct to rewrite frameworks in familiar languages is misguided when learning the original language would be far less effort than recreating the entire ecosystem.
